sentences.text: My name is Magda Blau, born Hellinger.
Spell the whole name-- M-A-G-D-A, B-L-A-U, H-E-double L-I-N-G-E-R.
The 19th of August, thousand-nine-hundred-sixteen.
I-- 19th of August, I, I became a 80-year-old woman.
I was born in Czechoslovakia in the town of Michalovce, which is spelled M-I-C-H-A-L-O-V-C-E, Michalovce.
Czechoslovakia to spell?
C-E-C-H-O-V-A-L-I-A.
Before the war, that means our younger years.
They've been very nice years, because we had a very nice neighborhood.
And a lot of Jewish girls and boys lived next to each other so we had a lot of company.

Even so, that we mixed with non-Jewish girls-- so much so that where I live in the fence, the father of the non-Jewish girl who lived next by made a door in the fence saw that his daughter or I could run in and out.
You don't have to cross the street.
And because we been such a bunch of girls, we always had activities.
On one place, we played theater.
On one place, we've been singing and dancing.
And on one place, we did different.
We've been always occupied.
And certain days, the boys joined with us, too.
So that was a, a very full life.
And I had a very special girlfriend whose name was Martha-- in Hebrew, Jaffa, J-A-double F-A.
We been about the same age.
She was two months older.
But she became a orphan because her father fell in the war.
The mother didn't want to survive without the father, so she died by starvation.
The same thing, the grandmother couldn't take that-- she died.
And so she became a orphan with the old grandfather.
So our family looked after her.
And so we brought up like two sister, so much so just show you a sample.
When we been teenagers, we promised each other then when we marry and we will have a daughter, we will give her a name Maya, which means M-A-Y-A.
Because my Hebrew name was [?
Malka. ?] This is M-A.
Her was Jaffa-- J-F.
And so our daughter name is Maya Lee, as you know.
And her daughter was Eva Maya.
They all have two names.
So our children years been very, very happy and very nice.
We had wonderful parents.
And they was good-hearted and helpful to everybody.
And there was lot of women widows.
And they knew where to, to come that they need something.
Because we had a huge garden where everything was growing.
So those people came.
And Mom said, go on and get yourself whatever you need-- vegetables, fruit.
And then come in.
And I will give you fat, meat, so you have-- what is that.
When Jewish holidays came, or even Friday night, our father wrote always somebody for the dinner was there a stranger or visitor or somebody who need it.
And when it was the [INAUDIBLE] high holidays, always some widows and the children participated with our family.
Must have a little water.
[DRINKS]
Beg your pardon?
Yeah.
My father name was Igna.
I should spell it?
Igna [?
Isak ?] Hellinger.
My mother's name was Berta Bracha Hellinger.
And we had, from my father, the fa-- the father a grandfather and grandmother living in our home town.
And I was, from childhood, so close to the families that my little girlfriend, every Saturday, we went to visit every Hellinger.
Because the whole big part of the city was Hellingers.
So we went from He-- one Hellinger to another to say happy Shabbat till we came to our grandparents, which they lived on the other side of the town.
Later, our grandfather was sick.
But I, as a young child, every day visited him.
And he said, I have so many grandchildren.
But you are the only one.
And my name is Magda.
So he called [?
Makala ?] who came to me, God bless you.
And so that, that was also love from every side.
It was, they-- my father letted build.
We builded to our things.
So it was very comfortable.
And it had a few more rooms that we needed.
So it was time that Saturday, the Jewish children didn't travel home.
And because my father was very well-known, so the children was billeted at our place from Friday till Sunday.
So that's why we had a lot of room there.
Yes, yes.
I had four brothers.
And I was the fifth girl.
Yeah.
My older brother was Maximilian Mordecai.
My second brother was Ernest [?
Orzer. ?] My third brother was Eugene-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I think a Hebrew name [?
Yacov. ?] I can't remember.
And the youngest was Artur-- Hebrew name [?
Ari. ?] We been a close-knit brothers.
But mostly, I was together with the younger brother.
Because the other brothers went to learn early morning Hebrew or Hebrew his-- or Jewish history.
They went to cheder that you call that.
The early morning, the little children if it snow or cold, they went to learn.
My father was a teacher.
He taught Jewish history in the whole town in every school.
So he was busy from eight o'clock till five o'clock in the afternoon, from eight till nine, from nine till ten.
That's how it was.
He just came 12:00 home for dinner and then went back.
And he was very busy.
Except that because he had that knowledge, a good knowledge of German, so he taught some-- it was very much in, in fashion to know German.
And some, he taught German.
And also, there was some people then which couldn't write and read.
And they've been grown-up people.
And they've been very bashful.
But they knew they can come to my father and nobody will knew.
And he taught them to read, to write.
So he was very known.
Even wherever I traveled, they all remembered my father-- generous, knowledgeable, and lovely person he was.
She would-- in summer, she would wake up 5:00 and take the maid and went to the garden.
Because they had from potatoes till corn till tomatoes, everything was growing in the garden.
So everything has a season when you have to put it in or take it out.
So she was busy.
And then she was cooking.
She was baking her own bread and challah and everything.
And and here we been the children.
She looked.
And all the neighbors came from her to learn how to cook and how to bake.
So she had a busy day, too.
She had a busy day, very busy.
Because Friday, there came six boys who stayed till Sunday morning.
So already, with six people more to feed-- Friday night, Saturday, and till Sunday morning-- so only that, it's additionally to the family of, of seven, another six.
I went to four elementary school.
And then as I was quite young when I went to make the gymnasium, which is like a high school.
But then, being a ardent Zionist, so they said there more important to things like just to study.
We have to study those subject which was good and needed in Israel.
That's why I went after finishing those four high school, I went to study that economy school when they taught you about food, about plants, about those thing to know for agriculture and that.
Yeah.
I was-- every time I did a year.
First of all, I finished that.
Then I was too young to be finished.
So I went to study another type of high school.
And then I went to that economy school.
And then I finished that, I was very occupied with working for Zionism, starting new organizations, and doing always some like a Purim party, Hanukkah party.
It was all bringing together money for [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], which I was the best worker in town.
I bought the most money up by these functions plus my private work.
The organization was Hashomer Hatzair.
Many years, many years-- because I was sent out to start new organizations from a city where nothing was.
I was sent out to start on my own.
And I build it up.
I was very capable and had the idea how to, how to do it, to be-- [INAUDIBLE].
Very unusual, because the religious was very against that organization.
But I did it anyway.
Because that is not a religious organization.
My father understood it, even so that he was a Orthodox.
But he wasn't a fanatic.
And he believed that we need a country.
And that's the only way to prepare the young ones the want to go to Palestine, which lot of young people then went, luckily.
Because then came at time that they couldn't.
And we couldn't who stayed home.
I had a certificate to go.
But a girl asked very much that I should give it to her.
You are a capable girl.
You are a good-looking good.
I am ugly.
I am poor.
I am this and that.
What chance I have?
Give to me the certificate.
I gave her the certificate.
Do you remember her name?
No.
But I see her before me.
[LAUGHS] She was very dark skinned and dark hair, small and skinny.
And in this [NON-ENGLISH], in this way, there were went a very handsome fellow who was in love with me.
But she said-- so I said, how you want to do it?
I will be there next to him.
I will be good to him so long that he will get accustomed to me.
And that's what happened.
In Czechoslovakia?
Very well, very well-- because with the high authorities, they had a very good contact.
And my father was in contact with a very high priest there, which was a very well-known.
And he had prestige in the city.
And he said, with my father, that Jews and Christians should live next to each other without hating [INAUDIBLE] them.
And that helped, you know?
And, and also we knew personally mayor of the city, so much so that his son wasn't a good student.
I was.
So he arranged that even which wasn't a custom there his son sitting with me at the same bench.
When the professor came in, so he said to him, go to your place.
And he said, this is my place.
Because all the boys were sitting there.
The girls were sitting here.
And one boy was sitting there next to me.
Yeah, that was there all boys together.
But that helped a lot.

And also, the very highest like-- there was our neighbor, the like, like would say, like a premiere-- not just the mayor of the city but premiere of the whole surrounding-- like Jeff Kennett-- so was our neighbor.
Beg pardon?
Yeah.
I don't know.
I was so blind that I didn't feel it.
Because we been scouts, Jewish scout.
And we been friendly with the non-Jewish scout.
So we been so involved with non-Jewish, we didn't think that something can happen, that something can happen here.
But it did happen.
And I had a kindergarten teacher.
And the head of the-- it was like not-- they didn't call it SS.
They called it Hlinka Guard.
Hlinka was the name of a fascist.
In Slovakia.
Beg pardon?
I?
No.
I had a-- I studied for being a kindergarten teacher.
When I finished, I opened a kindergarten.
And it was a private kindergarten.
Because there wasn't any kindergarten in the town.
So it was a private.
But it was affiliated to the state government schools.
Mostly Jewish but some non-Jewish, because the better class non-Jewish came to the kindergarten.
Yeah.
And you know what?
The mayor of the city helped me to get the, the building where was the rooms for the kinder-- he helped me.
And also with the furniture, because here remember, before the war, there was kindergarten.
And we knew there where the furniture is.
So he arrange the furniture, I should have the furniture, too.
So that's why I couldn't realize that it can something happen.
And like I, I said, the top man of that Hlinka Guard, he was a shoemaker.
And he made shoe for me for the last few years.
And he had his shop on the way when I went to the kindergarten.
Every morning when I went, he came out, he said hello.
But he lived in our neighborhood.
So I thought it's all right.
One day, he came to the kindergarten.
And he said, you know they will be taking away the Jewish girl, girls.
But if you want, I arrange it for you that one of the children will be as your child.
And then you don't have to go with the girls.
And then you can stay.
And I will protect you.
Yeah.
And I said, why, why would you do that?
And what happens?
So he, he said, they are going to take the girls away.
But he didn't say where.
But then they said that it's to [INAUDIBLE] factory.
Then we thought it's not such a big thing.
No, we-- I don't know.
That's why I blame-- I don't know whom to blame-- that probably in the world, everybody knew.
But in our town, nobody knew and talked about it.
Only in the last few days, some girls traveled to Hungary.
Or they hid.
Or they gotted themself a non-Jewish papers.
And they went in hiding.
Beg pardon?
I didn't hear.
At all, because I was in the morning, from morning till lunch, till one o'clock.
And I arranged everything.
And then I prepared some-- because I myself was writing stories and had a the theater, you know, the-- so I prepared material.
So I was busy.
And then I had friends and going to the picture theater.
And I was altogether blind.
My brother, the oldest, was in Israel.
The middle one was like as a soldier in uniform.
Later, the last few weeks, they didn't-- they took away their military uniform.
And they just gave them-- I don't remember-- blue or green uniform.
And they send them to a labor camp.
But that wasn't so bad.
They were there some.
And it still wasn't visible how dangerous it was.
No.
But the-- in the last few days, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a man who was a, a conductor on the train, conductor, he told somebody that he went with the train.
And he heard that they are taking people to the concentration camp and what going what's happening there.
So he said that to his girlfriend.
And straight away, he took her away from the city.
And I don't know.
But she survived without going to the concentration camp.
I don't know.
I-- when I heard that people are hiding and people are going to Hungary in the last few weeks.
This was what period of time?
Can you remember the date?
It must have be in January, '42.
But I don't know why I didn't-- I didn't like to hide.
I didn't like to go to Hungary.
I don't know.
Because then when we heard something that it was to go to [INAUDIBLE] factory.
So I said, why should I hide?
Is that so bad?
So we will work in [INAUDIBLE] factory.
And the brother, when they started to hear something, so they went to the woods to fight with the partisans because fight against the Germans.
But they didn't know about that they will take us away.
But then--
Yeah?
When my brother came home for a, a like, holiday, that day, they came to pick up our family.
First, they picked me up.
So I went with, with the-- to the train, and with the train--
They-- that, and that man did it, you know, who, who wanted me to be his girlfriend or a-- a lover.
And because I refused, the same day, he sent from the Hlinka Guard, I should come to the city hall.
